sn56.36:1.1: “Monks, suppose that a man were to cut down all the grass, sticks, branches, & leaves in India and to gather them into a heap.
sn56.36:1.2: Having gathered them into a heap, he would make stakes from them,
sn56.36:1.3: and having made stakes he would impale all the large animals in the sea on large stakes, all the medium-sized animals in the sea on medium-sized stakes, & all the minute animals in the sea on minute stakes.
sn56.36:1.4: Before he had come to the end of all the sizable animals in the sea,
sn56.36:2.1: all the grass, sticks, branches, & leaves here in India would have been used up and exhausted.
sn56.36:2.2: It wouldn’t be feasible for him to impale on stakes the even-more-numerous minute animals in the sea.
sn56.36:2.4: Because of the minuteness of their bodies.
sn56.36:2.5: So great is the plane of deprivation.
sn56.36:2.6: “Freed from this great plane of deprivation is the individual consummate in view, who discerns, as it has come to be, that ‘This is stress … This is the origination of stress … This is the cessation of stress … This is the path of practice leading to the cessation of stress.’
sn56.36:3.1: “Therefore your duty is the contemplation, ‘This is stress … This is the origination of stress … This is the cessation of stress.’ Your duty is the contemplation, ‘This is the path of practice leading to the cessation of stress.’”
